data.title: Lumentum: AI's Hidden Bottleneck Winner
data.description:
Lumentum Holdings Inc. (LITE) is quite an attractive investment. The company is operating in one of the most important AI infrastructure chains, where the demand grows due to physical network restrictions and not a short-term hype. At the same time, its results depend on a few big clients and difficult manufacturing scaling.

Why Now?

AI infrastructure is transitioning. Large-scale AI clusters connect thousands of GPUs, and their effectiveness not only depends on their compute power but also on how fast the data is moving. I believe that the current transition from 800G to 1.6T optical interconnects is not only an upgrade but now a necessary condition for next-generation models to work without network bottlenecks. Copper connections at these data rates become obsolete due to energy costs, heat, and signal degradation, so the optics become the only scalable alternative.

I think that with this stock we get a picks and shovels situation. Everyone talks about AI models and microchips, but without data transfer infrastructure, those GPUs are just waiting for work. Optical transceivers, lasers, and optical switching solutions allow AI systems to work as one platform, not like a separate pile of servers. From the research I have done to analyze this company, I have found that high-speed optics demand is growing, OCS orders backlog is growing, and product mix is moving toward higher-value solutions, which have better margins. This signals to me that clients are planning for long-term commitments.

Also, let's not forget about the broader context. The current market environment is challenging: after huge CAPEX plans were reported by many companies, investors are trying to understand whether it is good or bad and whether it's going to pay off. In my opinion, it is not a good opportunity to buy big tech companies or indexes as they are risky. The market seems to be looking for picks and shovels, not gold anymore. Stocks like LITE perform well in this environment, even though they correlate with the broader tech sector; at least you buy quality stock.

Q2 FY2026 Results

In my opinion, the latest results are exactly what the market was expecting from an AI infrastructure provider - a beat and raise. Revenue beat the expectations, and most importantly, management increased guidance, showing that the demand is not a one-time jump. Growth was mainly driven by Cloud and Networking segments, where hyperscaler orders remain strong. This is important, as the market is currently very sensitive to any bad signals about AI or infrastructure and could cause a sell-off really quickly.

However, for me, the revenue was not that important, but margin expansion is. Profitability and operating margins expanded due to the product mix being focused on higher-value optical solutions. This is a classic operating leverage effect: when volumes are increasing and fixed costs remain almost the same, every additional dollar of revenue increases profit. I believe this dynamic will remain, and we will see that EPS can grow faster than revenue.

Now let's stay skeptical. Q2 FY26 GAAP EPS was $0.89; at the same time, non-GAAP EPS was $1.67. The difference is $0.78 per share, almost half of the profits. The biggest correction here is stock-based compensation (around $48.8 million that quarter), which is a non-cash expense today, but in the long term it means dilution for current shareholders. Also, quite significant was intangible assets amortization, which happened due to their previous acquisitions. No red flags, to be fair; everything is standard for a growing technology company, but it's good to understand that part of that profit appears due to accounting adjustments. In the first six months of FY2026, operating cash flow was $184.6 million, when GAAP net income was $82.4 million. This shows that profit is not only on paper, but the business is actually generating cash and that many of the adjustments I mentioned are non-cash.

Their balance sheet is very interesting and may seem complicated. The company has around $1.16 billion in cash and short-term investments but also around $3.2 billion in convertible bonds. You can notice a "substantial doubt" warning in their report. It happened due to technical factors, such as the share price going up so much that the bonds became convertible; due to this reason, in the report they wrote it as short-term obligations. This creates an artificial working capital deficit, but from my experience, creditors like to convert shares rather than ask the company for money.

Moat And Competition

I think that Lumentum’s strength is not only their product but also how hard it is to make one and replace it; the harder it is to do so, the higher the barrier to entry competition has, which explains their valuation premium. The company works with InP technology, which allows them to reach high optical speeds and efficiency. Here the most important thing is the manufacturing process and yield management; to me, it's black magic, which competitors could copy only in theory. In practice, a high yield level means lower costs and more reliable components. When Hyperscalers are engineering data centers, they depend on established providers, as any instability in the network layer could cost millions.

The competitive environment is quite busy. Coherent (COHR) and Broadcom (AVGO) also work in optical components and data center connections, while module producers in Asia are competing with their prices. Lumentum is differentiating itself by controlling the most important chains - from laser sources to high-speed optical solutions and OCS technology. This vertical integration allows them to optimize performance and energy efficiency. To clients, this means better signal integrity and lower energy consumption. In other words, they are not only selling a component but also performance.

However, a strong relationship with hyperscalers has its own drawbacks. Significant revenue concentration among a few clients means that one big customer cycle could strongly affect the results. I have held stocks in the past that had one client adjust their budget, and the stock dropped, so this risk is quite significant. This creates visibility and long-term contracts but, at the same time, increases cyclicality risk. If the CAPEX momentum we see today decreases, the revenue could start fluctuating faster than in more diversified sectors.

Catalysts In Next 6 Months

There are some catalysts for the next few months that could be important for overall sentiment. For example, the OFC conference in March, which is like the Super Bowl, is just for optical technology players. I expect that the company will demonstrate next-generation 1.6T and even 3.2T solutions. If this technology gets enough attention and positive industry response, it could strongly affect the narrative.

Another strong catalyst could be Q3 results in May. The management is expecting 85% YoY growth, so the expectations are high. The market most likely will react to whether they beat or miss expectations, so it is very important to look at whether the margins remain strong.

Any announcement about refinancing or settling convertible notes would remove that "going concern" badge and could be viewed positively. Still, I do not believe it would be as important to the market as the two above.

Risks

The biggest structural risk is client concentration. The company’s revenue is very dependent on a few technology giants, so strategic decisions on their part could have a disproportionate effect on results. If, let's say, one big hyperscaler decided to change optical switching architecture or lower investment pace, order flow could significantly shrink. Similarly, in the 3D sensor business, big orders from one client could become small very fast if the cycle changes.

The second risk is operations. Manufacturing expansion in Thailand is happening aggressively, mainly to satisfy growing demand. Such ramp-ups are always difficult, so we might see some yield fluctuations, component supply disruptions, or commodity shortages, like indium phosphide shortages, which could put pressure on margins and affect delivery timelines.

Liquidity risk, the one I mentioned before, is more technical than real; still, let's not ignore it. Convertible bond classification in short-term obligations creates pressure on the balance sheet, which I mentioned should not become a real issue as the creditors will most likely convert into shares, creating a dilution problem. If the share price dropped or refinancing opportunities disappeared, this would become a real cash flow problem. Still, I do not believe we will see this scenario.

Valuation

If we compare with other optical network and data infrastructure competitors, Lumentum stock is traded at a clear premium. Forward P/E shows that the company is more expensive than Coherent (COHR), Ciena (CIEN), or Pure Storage (PSTG), and the EV/Sales ratio is significantly higher than traditional network equipment suppliers. At first sight, a valuation like this seems aggressive, especially if we take into account profitability, which is just transitioning to a higher margin phase.

However, I do not believe that the market is valuing Lumentum as a traditional component manufacturer but rather like a critical AI infrastructure layer supplier. As GPU clusters scale and move to 800G and 1.6T optical solutions, the density of optical connections per server is increasing rapidly. This expands the market they address and allows optical solutions to become not just a cost component in quarterly results but the main performance condition.

Due to this reason, Lumentum should be compared to higher-growth infrastructure leaders in my view, not its traditional cyclical equipment manufacturers. Premium valuation just reflects market expectations that the company will become a leading player.

Summary

For me, the company looks like a classical infrastructure winner in AI. The company is operating in a critical data flow layer, where demand comes from physical bandwidth constraints. The Cloud and Networking segments are pushing this growth; margins are expanding due to higher value products, and OCS and next-generation optics create structural growth potential. While yes, the risks exist - clients concentration, manufacturing scaling, and high market expectations. If the market is going to be favorable, I believe we will see growth. Despite high expectations and premium valuation, I am very bullish about Lumentum's role in the AI infrastructure sector. In my opinion, long-term upside outweighs near-term valuation risks. I am rating the stock as a strong buy.

data.title: Inflation Solved? I'm Not Buying It
data.description:
Despite last week being packed with headlines, the broader markets remained relatively flat. The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100 were down low single digits in the week, and the Dow Jones remained flat.

One of the main headlines during the week was the January CPI report. The headline CPI cooled more than what the Street anticipated, suggesting that the U.S. economy is losing momentum. On the day of the CPI release, yields dipped on the idea that easier policy is back on the table. However, I don't think we are out of the woods yet.

A big chunk of the headline relief came from energy (a volatile item). Core and services remained sticky, and consumers are still feeling inflationary pressures.

Overall, despite the January decline in unemployment, I still think we could see as many as four rate cuts this year once Warsh takes over Powell's mandate at the Fed. That's a nice tailwind for H2. Until then, I see many risks that could lead to a correction, including the Congress ruling on tariffs.

In line with my last coverage on the macro, I remain cautious.

An Intense Week in The Markets

I was quite surprised to see the headline CPI growing at only 0.2% m/m, down to 2.4% y/y from the 2.7% in December last year. I think I was not the only one surprised here, as the Street was looking for 0.3% m/m and 2.5% y/y.

While Treasury yields fell after the CPI release to 4.075%, as the data supports further rate cuts, I am not particularly bullish on this CPI print.

Why?

First, expectations for a rate cut before the end of Powell's term are still low, despite the economy running relatively cold (more on this in a moment).

This week, the President of the Federal Reserve Bank of Cleveland, Beth Hammack, noted that "we could be on hold for quite some time". I think that “quite some time” likely runs until the new Fed Chair takes over in May this year. After that, my view lines up with Goldman’s Jonny Fine, who projected four cuts this year, starting in June.

Second, Energy was a clear disinflation driver, down 1.5% m/m, with gasoline prices down 3.2% m/m.

This disinflation is driven by low crude prices during the period, mainly supported by a strong global supply, pressuring prices. Based on the projections from EIA (see below), I believe we could see further disinflation in energy this year, with a 5.8% sequential decline in gasoline prices forecasted by the EIA in 2026.

Core inflation didn't cool the same way as the headline CPI. The print came in line with the consensus, at 0.3% m/m and 2.5% y/y. Notably, services (excluding energy) rose +0.4% m/m vs. 0.3% in December, with some sticky inflation in airline fares, up 6.5% m/m.

I am now monitoring the U.S. Jets ETF, which has recently broken new highs since 2021. The inflation in airline fares, coupled with the lower fuel prices, could be an early indication of strong airline results in Q1 this year.

As noted in the NY Fed Survey of Consumer Expectations, consumers still feel inflationary pressures, and the 1-year inflation expectation is 3.1%, significantly above the Fed's 2% target.

Moving on, the January employment report was the second highlight of the week, with the unemployment rate dropping to 4.3% from the 4.5% in December. Interestingly enough, the January Employment Report pushed the 10-year yield up by 4.5 bps to 4.19% right after the report was released. I think this was an overreaction, given that nonfarm payrolls came in at +130k vs. the consensus of +70k.

As I said before, I’m not worried in the slightest about rate cuts in the second half of the year. In fact, I wouldn't completely rule out a jumbo cut (that is, 50 bps) in the June meeting, likely driven by Trump’s pressure on Warsh to lower interest rates.

The other two economic reports released this week, retail sales and housing, were a mere reflection of the CPI. Retail sales came in considerably below consensus, with headline retail sales flat m/m vs. the expected 0.4%. Core retail sales (the GDP-relevant metric) declined by -0.1% m/m, and the November data for this group was revised downwards from 0.4% to 0.2%.

On housing, I was highly disappointed with the January report, and I am now less bullish on the affordability trade, a topic I discussed in this article. Existing home sales came in at 3.91M vs. the consensus of 4.18M. Looking at the change, home sales are down 8.4% m/m.

What makes me sweat about housing is the fact that sellers are still reluctant to bite the bullet after overpaying in the 2020-21 real estate boom. Eventually, I think reality will come back to Earth for sellers. That’s when the affordability trade likely lifts off for homebuilders and mortgage lenders.

In the meantime, the median home price in the U.S. in January 2026 was $396,800, the highest for any January, representing an increase of 0.9% y/y. I simply can’t wrap my head around how home prices keep going up while home sales keep falling.

Finally, tariffs became a macro factor again after the House passed a measure this week to end Trump’s emergency tariffs on Canada. Some reports also noted that the administration was considering scaling back some steel/aluminum tariffs. The focus is now on the Supreme Court tariff case, with February 20 as the next scheduled opinion day. The tariffs at issue are mainly grouped into two buckets:

  • The fentanyl tariffs on China, Canada, and Mexico.
  • The broader reciprocal tariffs, tied to a declared emergency around trade deficits

If these are struck down, the U.S. government could be on the hook for $150B in refunds. I would closely monitor any developments in the Supreme Court tariff case, as I think the risk of a panic selloff related to tariffs is not off the table this year.

Conclusion

The economic data this week was mixed.

On the positive side (positive = rate cuts), inflation cooled more than expected in January, and the market immediately treated that as fuel for lower yields and easier policy later this year. However, I wouldn't pop a bottle of champagne just yet.

A meaningful chunk of the CPI headline disinflation came from energy, and, in my view, the Fed won’t pivot off one month of noise when services inflation is still running hot. In fact, core inflation, especially services, still looks stubborn, and the NY Fed Survey of Consumer Expectations shows that consumers aren’t buying the "inflation is solved" narrative.

On the negative side, the January jobs report doesn't back up further rate cuts until the next Chair takes over in May. Housing is still pressured, and consumers are still struggling with affordability.

Overall, I reiterate my hold rating on the broader markets, as I see further downside pressure ahead, especially if Congress takes down some of Trump's tariffs later this year.

data.title: DigitalBridge: The Unclear Future Of The Preferreds After The SoftBank Acquisition
data.description:
DigitalBridge (DBRG) is being acquired by Japanese investment holding company SoftBank Group (SFTBY) for $16 per share, in a deal that will see the asset manager become a subsidiary of the AI-obsessed SoftBank. The takeout price comes at a 150% premium to DBRG's 52-week low but at a broad discount to its 5-year trading range. For SoftBank, they gain access to an alternative asset management company focused on digital infrastructure, as demand for AI drives a data center boom. DBRG had $40.7 billion in fee-earning equity under management (“FEEUM”) as of the end of its last reported fiscal 2025 third quarter. I last covered DBRG in October.

This increased by $6.6 billion, around 19% on a year-over-year comp, on the back of organic capital formation from the launch of a new fund. Fee revenue was up 22% year-over-year to $93.5 million, with fee-related earnings (“FRE”) coming in at $37.3 million. FRE was up 43% over its year-ago comp, with SoftBank gaining access to a broadly cash-generative company with a growing assets-under-management profile. The big uncertainty with the deal is the treatment of DBRG's three outstanding preferred shares. These initially surged higher on rumors of a pending deal with SoftBank, with a rumored takeout price for the commons in the range of $25 to $35 per share, not realized.

The DBRG Preferreds Dip On Uncertainty

The preferreds have all dropped on the back of the acquisition because the language of the acquisition implies that they will remain outstanding. Further, there is a section that also implies that they might also be delisted from the NYSE at some point after the acquisition closes. There are two documents laying out this uncertain future for the DBRG preferreds, the first being the Form 8-K filed on 12/30/2025. This states that the preferreds will remain outstanding as a share of preferred stock of the surviving corporation. This explains the drop, as the change of control feature spelled out in the IPO prospectuses of the preferreds has not been activated.

The IPO prospectuses of the preferreds, the second document, spell out exactly what is defined as a change of control. DBRG, previously called Colony Capital, undertook a rebrand and transformation from a real estate investment trust (“REIT”) in 2021. The preferreds were a legacy from the Colony Capital days, with the IPO prospectus for the Series H (DBRG.PR.H) issued in 2015. The change of control feature in the prospectus is explained as the acquisition by any entity of more than 50% of the voting power of DBRG. Further, following the crossing of this threshold, the surviving entity must remain listed on a national stock exchange. This is meant to protect investors from possible delisting, with the loss of liquidity following such an event being damaging to holders of the preferreds.

DBRG may, at its option, redeem the preferreds after the first date on which such a change of control occurs. Hence, it seems as though the change of control clause for the preferreds is not ironclad, as it does not compel DBRG to redeem the preferreds at their $25 per share redemption value. SoftBank will be able to take control of the company and will likely continue paying the coupons in some form. The Form 8-K implies that DBRG intends to delist the preferreds. While this is not certain, the language of the Form 8-K states in its section 5.5 that the “parent shall use its reasonable best efforts” to delist the common and preferred shares from the NYSE as soon as reasonably practicable.

There are still a few uncertainties, and the actual outcome for the preferreds might just be a name change. This delisting would constitute a change of control and demand that the surviving entity redeem the preferreds. Overall, we know for certain that SoftBank does not intend to redeem the preferreds. The price action reflects this. The attraction of the preferreds comes from what's a $1.78 per share annual coupon, which is driving a 9.77% yield on cost. The common shares were paying a dividend of just $0.01 per share, annualized for a 0.26% dividend yield. This was far below alternative asset management peers like Apollo Global Management (APO) or Brookfield Asset Management (BAM).

Conclusion

DBRG's preferreds are not being redeemed with the SoftBank transaction, creating deep uncertainty around their future against Section 5.5 of the Form 8-K, which implies that the surviving entity will take action to delist them. A delisting would form the worst possible scenario. Hence, I have not taken a position here. I am neutral on the DBRG securities from a rating perspective.

data.title: Arista Networks: Expect Growth To Pick Up In The Second Half Of 2026
data.description:
Arista Networks, Inc. (ANET) guided a robust outlook for FY26 underpinned by a heightened capital investment cycle among cloud services providers. With capital investments across the major hyperscalers expected to increase by 76% in 2026, I believe Arista may realize durable growth that may accelerate in the back half of FY26. One major headwind that Arista may face is the memory chip shortage that may result in incremental pressure on gross margins in the coming quarters. Despite the near-term headwinds, I believe Arista will have the flexibility to adjust pricing to overcome these challenges while maintaining a strong growth trajectory. With an upbeat forecast for Arista, I am upgrading my rating to a Strong Buy with a price target of $210/share at 18.12x FY27 price/sales.

Arista Networks Operational Update

Arista reported a robust close to Q4’25 with 29% top-line growth driven by a broad mix across customers. Accordingly, the FY25 revenue mix derived from cloud services providers at 48%, enterprise and financials at 32%, and specialty providers customers at 20%, suggesting a similar concentration to the cloud titans as the previous fiscal year. As a result of the strong adoption of 800G and 1.6T switching technologies in FY25, management is doubling their expectations for AI networking revenue for FY26 to $3.25b.

One of the leading indicators for future growth is the substantial increase in capital outlays across the hyperscalers. Accordingly, I’m forecasting the cumulative capital spend for 2026 to be roughly $666b, a 76% increase from the previous year driven by increased investments for compute capacity. Management at Arista Networks indicated that there will likely be some lag tied to Arista’s revenue gains from this increase in capital spending, as networking equipment is typically sourced after the data center is constructed, power is secured, and GPUs and CPUs are sourced. Nonetheless, the increased capital outlay may be an indicator of strong growth for Arista in the coming quarters. Assuming some lag, I believe Arista could realize higher growth in 2H26 through FY27.

In addition to the cloud titans, more Neocloud operators are coming into play that may provide scale in time. One example of this is Riot Platforms (RIOT), which is converting bitcoin mining capacity into data center hosting services in its recently announced 10-year agreement with Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). The agreement is set for 25 MW of capacity that can scale to 200 MW. Though Arista’s management voiced some skepticism on the Q4 earnings call relating to the broader bitcoin mining industry shift, I believe partnerships with credible companies like AMD can drive confidence into this corner of the market, particularly if the agreement were to scale to 200 MW in capacity.

One of the challenges Arista may be faced with is the tight memory chip market. Some are expecting the memory chip shortage to last through 2027, which may lead to some pressure on Arista’s margins in FY26 despite setting the stage with $6.8b in purchase commitments, a 42% sequential increase. Looking ahead, Micron (MU) is in the process of constructing two new foundries to expand memory fabrication capacity, which may begin loosening up the supply chain in 2027-2028.

In Q4’25, Arista launched the Arista 7800R4 Series switch designed with 460 Tbps fabric capacity that can be used as data center interconnects (DCI) and AI spines. Like other networking switches, the 7800R4 Series runs on Arista EOS software.

On the campus networking side, Arista is expecting to achieve $1.25b in revenue for FY26, driven by Arista Virtual Ethernet Segment with Proxy ARP (VESPA) and Arista Autonomous Virtual Assistant AVA, which enables agentic AI. VESPA can be a critical component for scaling out networks, particularly as more connected devices are connected to the Wi-Fi networks. AVA essentially enables AI agents to interact with the network environment across multiple domains. The AI platform was designed to also enhance troubleshooting and continuous monitoring.

Arista Networks Financial Position

Looking ahead to Q1’26, I’m forecasting Arista to generate $2.62b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $0.84/share. Margins are expected to face incremental pressure in the quarter as a result of the potentially higher revenue mix for cloud titans, though I’m expecting improvements to the operating margin throughout FY26 as revenue scales with increased cloud spending, which I’m expecting to occur in 2H26.

Arista closed Q4 in a position of financial strength with $10.74b in cash & equivalents and marketable securities and no debt on the balance sheet, providing flexibility to continue to reinvest in operations or grow inorganically through M&A.

Risks Related To Arista Networks

Bull Case

The outlook for data center builds provides a robust growth market for Arista Networks for the back half of FY26 and into FY27. Momentum will likely continue as enterprise AI adoption continues to scale.

Bear Case

The memory chip shortage could impact growth capacity for Arista through 2027, potentially pressuring gross margins if the firm is pushed to absorb the costs. The shortage could also indirectly impact Arista if customers were to push out orders if GPU and CPU sourcing were to be delayed.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

Arista repurchased $620.1mm in shares in Q4’25 for a total of $1.6b for all of FY25. As of Q4, Arista has $817.9mm remaining under the existing share repurchase authorization. Management indicated on the earnings call that Arista will likely take an opportunistic approach to repurchasing shares going forward based on market dynamics and operations.

ANET shares are currently trading at 20.15x price/sales, a moderately appealing valuation considering the stock’s historical trading range. Given the optimistic outlook for continued top-line growth and the possibility of improving margins throughout FY26, I believe Arista shares may continue their growth trajectory to reflect the level of capital spending for data center capacity. I am upgrading my rating to a Strong Buy with a price target of $210/share at 18.12x FY27 price/sales.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: Structural Headwinds Persist And The Outlook Could Be Getting Even Worse For Nebius Investors
data.description:
The AI space continues to be perhaps the favorite area for retail investors to place their bets, despite recent potential changes in market sentiment, which could be showing signs of going in the opposite direction. Within this space, the neoclouds (specialized providers of AI-related compute) are a particularly popular category, with Nebius (NBIS), perhaps, being the most popular. Nebius faces significant headwinds going forward, though, in its efforts to create shareholder value. The earnings figures just reported offer no compelling reasons for hope, in my view. There is way too much risk here, and, as a result, investors should steer far away from this one.

Recapping Q4 Earnings

Here are the “Financial Highlights” management wants investors to focus on (after all, these are the summary figures they chose to put on the first page of the earnings release):

Nebius bulls like to highlight the insane revenue growth y/y, as well as the significant reduction in adjusted EBITDA loss. Additionally, they will highlight future expectations for ARR (annualized run-rate revenue). Finally, some even might highlight the flip to profitability in terms of net income.

However, all four of these perspectives are problematic. Let’s take each in turn.

First, the profitability on a net income basis is not due to anything that the company is doing operationally. Instead, this stems from supposed gains on their investment in the privately held company ClickHouse (see p. 10 of the previous Form 6-K):

It should go without saying that Nebius bulls shouldn’t pin their thesis on supposed gains in the value of an investment that is not publicly traded and whose value is therefore subject to significant debate. Of course, instead, they want to structure their thesis on the operations of the company itself, so let’s turn there.

The Nebius bulls will often point to improvements in adjusted EBITDA. However, I’m highly suspicious of this metric. As Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger have often warned investors, “EBITDA” is an arguably terribly useless metric, especially insofar as it has the tendency to add back multiple material costs of a business (costs that are especially important for a highly capital-intensive business like Nebius). And then when we make it an “adjusted” figure, the leeway and wiggle-room increase even more. To see what I mean, here is the operational definition management uses for “adjusted EBITDA”:

Instead of all the subjectivity and obfuscation that is potentially tied up in a Non-GAAP metric like “adjusted EBITDA,” I think it’s better to evaluate the company based on more “objective” GAAP figures like operating income, for example. As can be seen in the above income statement, the company increased their operating loss y/y by almost $200 million. It’s interesting how when we look at more "objective" GAAP figures like this, the picture tends to look less rosy.

Turning to revenues, the bulls always seem to want to use the “ARR” metric (“ARR” = “annualized run-rate revenue”). They oftentimes highlight stuff like the following:

All this looks very nice and confidence-inspiring, until we begin to critically assess it. ARR is defined as the most recent month’s revenue multiplied by twelve. For a company whose revenues are growing quickly, this has the effect of dramatically overstating actual annual revenues and leaving investors with the impression that numbers are much bigger than they actually are. For instance, per the above graphic, actual 2025 ARR was $1.25 billion; however, 2025 revenues (once again, a GAAP measurement) were $530 million. That means ARR is overstating actual revenues by more than a factor of 2. This reflects a problem that can come out of a growth investor mindset: there’s always a tendency to want to bring all the growth into the present, presumably out of recognition that growth expectations are risky - i.e., it’s hard to execute on and achieve those expectations. So, if we just use “ARR,” we can sort of squint and have all that growth right now.

No, I would, again, just encourage investors to stick to plain vanilla, boring GAAP measures like “revenue.” And why would Nebius bulls want to stray away from this metric? After all, the company has seen explosive growth y/y. Well, as I write about in my last article on Nebius, explosive revenue growth doesn’t necessarily mean explosive profitability growth and, thus, business value. And if the company cannot achieve operational leverage as it scales, there’s no hope for shareholder value creation.

Do We See Evidence of Shareholder Value Creation?

As I underscored in my previous article on Nebius, the company’s current financial operational metrics are unsustainable in the long term. The core operations are loss-making and burn immense amounts of cash. The Nebius bulls will tell you that this is fine; as soon as the company reaches a certain scale, they say, the profits will start flowing and everything will be great.

However, I am highly skeptical of this claim. From the latest shareholder letter:

"In Q4’25, capital expenditures were approximately $2.1 billion, primarily driven by purchases of GPUs and GPU-related hardware, and our data center expansion activities including investments in planned greenfield sites."

According to fellow SA contributor Yiannis Zourmpanos, close to 80% of capital expenditures go toward GPUs.

This is a big problem, in my view. These GPUs have a short refresh cycle, anywhere from potentially 6 years according to the most optimistic estimates, down to as little as 1-2 years according to the most pessimistic assessments (e.g., while the GPUs might still function after a year or two, they would, nevertheless, be obsolete due to new GPU releases and customers’ preference for the latest and greatest). Within this context, anyone who is resting a bullish thesis for NBIS on the supposition that the company will go through a high capex period of 3-4 years or something and, after that, they will turn off the capex spigot and just sit back and watch the money pour in, seems to be significantly off with their expectations of the future to come here, in my opinion.

Instead, the company seems to be on track for high capital expenditures, of the sort we’re currently seeing, far far into the future. And what do these capital expenditures look like? Here are the figures from the cash flow statement:

While cash flows from operations increased by ~$140 million y/y, capital expenditures increased by ~$3.2 billion!! This is horrendous, especially when measured against the backdrop of the view that free cash flow (i.e., cash flows from operations minus capex) is the best indicator of shareholder value creation (a widely shared view). If this is the sort of future shareholders have to look forward to due to the necessity to constantly refresh data centers with high-cost cutting-edge GPUs, I don’t see how things turn out well here for shareholders at all.

When we look at things in terms of operational leverage based on current data, things look very concerning as well. While the company did see explosive revenue growth of 479%, growing revenues by $438.3 million, they also saw explosive growth in operating expenses, with operating expenses growing by $634.9 million. The NBIS bull thesis crucially hinges on the company achieving operational leverage as it scales; revenues need to be growing faster than operational expenses. However, the current operational data shows that not only is this not happening, but the precise opposite is happening.

And for anyone who claims, “Oh, it’ll all magically flip one day,” I would say, “What evidence do you have for this beyond hopes and dreams for the future?” All the evidence points to the idea that this will be a highly-capital intensive business far far into the future with massive depreciation costs due to constant GPU refresh cycles. Furthermore, while costs of AI-related compute on a per-token basis are coming down, the number of tokens being used in AI-related queries is dramatically increasing due to changes in how new models operate and are being used, with the net result being that, far from total AI compute costs going down, AI compute costs actually seem to be going up.

This is not a business model that promises shareholder value creation. In fact, I don’t see how anyone could argue that this business model is likely to produce anything but shareholder value destruction. This is a highly speculative business whose prospects hinge on flawless execution on growth expectations. Furthermore, there’s not any compelling reason to think that, even if that growth materializes, it will create any shareholder value whatsoever in the form of profits - again, due to the lack of a compelling reason to expect the emergence of operational leverage over time.

Stock Price Volatility Since My Last Article

The incredibly speculative nature of the company is reflected in the stock price movements since my last article on Nebius. The price swings and volatility have been absolutely insane, with NBIS shares going as high as $108.68/share on January 12, 2026, intraday, only to sink back down by more than 32% less than a month later to as low as $73.52/share intraday on February 5, 2026.

This reflects the massively speculative nature of this company and its stock. What this wild volatility tells me is that the market has no idea how to accurately value this company and assess its future prospects. Instead, investors seem to be just trading off vibes, momentum, and the like.

So, for these sorts of reasons, I would submit my contention that nobody really has much of an idea of what this company’s stock price is going to do over the short-term (say, the next 3-6 months or so). But, in the long-term, for the reasons outlined above, I think the stock is one long-term investors ought to stay very far away from. When the market shifts from being a voting machine over the period of months and turns into a weighing machine over the period of years, my strong expectation is that 3-5 years down the road, for instance, NBIS share price is going to be far less than what we’re seeing today.

Recent Developments in the AI-Space and in Markets

And developments in the AI-world and in markets are already starting to show indications in this direction. In recent months, collectively, investors have seemed to be taking more of a skeptical view of the future of AI and of the financial prospects of companies playing in this space as compared to the sort of optimism that we were seeing even as recently as September and October of last year.

Most notably, this has come in reaction to the proposed 2026 capex numbers by hyperscalers in their most recent earnings reports. Despite otherwise glowing updates on these businesses' operations, investors still largely punished the stocks of hyperscalers, in large part due to the massive increases in 2026 capex expectations. Investors seem to be collectively growing impatient with all this spending and are now, more and more, demanding to see compelling evidence of ROI.

Furthermore, Nvidia (NVDA) has not really seen much movement in its stock price at all in the wake of all these announcements of massive increases in capex, despite the fact that they would be the primary beneficiary. Here, investors seem to be growing doubtful of how sustainable all this spending is in the long-term and, thus, the degree to which Nvidia will be seeing such massive growth in the windfall it receives from all this spending as compared to what it has seen up until now.

This is all very bad for NBIS investors. If the current frenzy in AI infrastructure spending cools off due to investor concern about ROI, it could throw a massive monkey wrench into Nebius’s plans to scale rapidly over the next many years. And, furthermore, when we note that the perceived value attributed to NBIS by investors is premised on rapid growth expectations, anything which could stall that growth, even a little bit, is highly problematic.

Another such potential issue that could stall execution on growth expectations is power. Along with concerns about ROI, power is perhaps the most existential threat to the growth expectations investors have for NBIS and other players in this space. There seem to be no revolutionary fixes to the issues around power availability as of yet, but NBIS, all the other neoclouds, and all the hyperscalers are going to need some kind of revolutionary fix if they are going to be able to execute on the growth expectations they have set forth thus far.

Putting It All Together

So, with Nebius, we seem to have a highly capital-intensive business with no obvious path to profitability. The most obvious, Occam’s razor-style expectation for their future business prospects would seem to be that the business will create significant shareholder value destruction. This is evident in looking at their financial statements and in thinking in a sober way about the most likely course the business will take in its evolution over time. Nothing in the most recent quarterly results changes these expectations; on the contrary, it simply reifies them.

Risks to Thesis

Nebius could scale and execute at exactly (or in excess of) the insanely high rate investors are currently expecting. Furthermore, they could prove operational leverage over time, contrary to any current reasonable indications or bases for expectations in this regard. Additionally, hyperscalers could eventually prove compelling ROI on all their AI-related capex, contrary to anything we’re seeing up until this point, thus providing the runway to even make possible the insane growth expectations investors are imputing to Nebius.

Sure, all these things could happen. Anything could happen. However, betting money that they will happen, and thereby saying that they are the most likely course of events, seems highly suspect in my view for all the reasons I’ve tried to articulate here. Put another way, as the title of my last Nebius article states: “Nebius Epitomizes What's Wrong With Extremely High-Risk Hyper-Growth Investing.”

Investor Takeaway

I think there’s manifestly far too much risk with Nebius, and the warning signs are far too obvious. If you want to gamble, go to a casino. If you want to invest, look elsewhere; don’t put your hard-earned cash in a highly risky neocloud like Nebius. Recommendation: ‘strong sell.’

data.title: Aquestive Therapeutics: Biology Validated, Packaging Pending - A Forensic Analysis Of The Anaphylm CRL
data.description:
Executive Summary: The Asymmetric Alpha in Regulatory Clarity

The FDA issued a Complete Response Letter, CRL to Aquestive Therapeutics' New Drug Application for Anaphylm, a sublingual epinephrine film formulation, recently. Rather than raising any issues about efficacy, safety, or manufacturing of this drug, the FDA's concerns were related to minor issues related to Human Factors Engineering, specifically patients's interaction with the packaging and interaction clarity, and a supportive Pharmacokinetic, PK study to validate them.

Rather than a permanent setback, we view this news as a 12-month delay in Anaphylm's approval. Our probabilistic forecasting shows an 84.5% probability that the company resubmits NDA by Q3, 2026, and a 99.2% probability of resubmission within 12 months, with a projected approval window of January 2027 to May 2027.

We see this pullback as a contrarian buying opportunity with a Strong Buy rating and a sum-of-the-parts discounted cash flow (SOTP DCF) valuation of $7/share or 92% upside (12-month timeframe).

1. The Anaphylaxis Landscape: A Market Ripe for Disruption

The Problem:

Epinephrine is the antidote for histamine, the mediator released during anaphylaxis. Epinephrine autoinjectors are the current standard of care for treating anaphylaxis; however, they are expensive and bulky to carry, so 83% of patients fail to carry them. Moreover, liquid epinephrine needs to be stored between 20 and 25°C to prevent degradation. The device is complex to administer for bystanders in the case of an anaphylaxis emergency. Recently, an intranasal spray formulation of epinephrine, Neffy from ARS Pharmaceuticals (SPRY), was approved; however, it is still bulky to carry in one's pocket and has warnings about nasal pathology.

Anaphylm's Advantage:

Anaphylm is a sublingual formulation of epinephrine, prepared using the company's PharmFilm technology. The company already has an approved diazepam sublingual film for treating acute seizures. Anaphylm is postal stamp-sized and is convenient to carry in one's wallet or pocket. It is stable through temperature ranges from 70 C (158 F) to freezing temperatures and does not need to be refrigerated. It has no warnings about nasal pathology like Neffy.

The global epinephrine market was $2.48 billion/year in 2025 and is projected to reach $5.46 billion by 2034. In the company's surveys, 80% of patients prefer a non-injectable method, and 95% are interested in the film-dosing option. 85% of prescribers are interested in a film-dosing solution.

2. Stochastic Modeling: Stress-Testing the Timeline (Monte Carlo Simulation)

The key parameter we want to forecast is the time for NDA resubmission. We used a 1000 Monte Carlo simulations method to model the probability of NDA resubmission and FDA approval.

2.1 Model Inputs: Defining the Variables

We modeled the Total Time to Launch ($T_{launch}$) by treating each step of the regulatory process as an independent variable with its own probability distribution.

We modeled the 'Time to launch or T_launch' by considering each step of the FDA regulatory step as an independent variable with its own probability distribution. We input 'Time to hold a Type A meeting with the FDA' as T_proto (minimum 1 month, mode 2 months, and maximum 3 months). Other inputs in the model include: Time to run Human Factors Study (T_HF) with Beta-PERT distribution (Beta_PERT): optimistic 2 months, most likely 3 months, and pessimistic 5 months; and Time to run PK study (T_PK) with Beta_PERT: optimistic 2 months, most likely 3 months, and pessimistic 4 months (both studies are expected to be run simultaneously). Beta-PERT is the gold standard for project management risk, which allowed us to forecast the most likely outcome while accounting for fat tail risks like patient recruitment delays. We input FDA review clock (T_review or FDA review period after resubmission) with a discrete distribution (90% probability of a standard 6-month review under class 2 resubmission while accounting for a 10% risk of a major amendment, which would extend the review beyond 9 months).

2.2 Simulation Output: Quantifying the Certainty

The result of our modeling shows that the FDA CRL is primarily an issue of delay in Anaphylm approval, not a risk to its efficacy or safety. The results show that the risk of a multi-year delay is negligible (<1%). The median remediation time is just 6.8 months. 99.2% of simulations show a resubmission within 12 months (resubmission by Q3 2026 in 84.5% simulations) with a projected approval window between Jan 2027 to May 2027 (95% confidence interval).

3. Valuation Deep Dive: Sum-of-the-Parts (SOTP) Analysis

We used an SOTP DCF method to value the Aquestive pipeline. Inputs: a discount rate of 11.5%, a beta of 1.2, an equity risk premium of 6%, and an income tax rate of 21%.

Anaphylm:

We input the 2027 U.S. launch, a peak 18% market share, and 90% probability of success (PoS). We expect a rapid revenue ramp, reaching a peak of $650 million/year by 2033.

Anaphylm Free Cash Flow Forecast (Risk-Adjusted)

Year 2027E 2028E 2029E 2030E 2031E 2032E 2033E Terminal
Revenue ($M) 35 95 185 310 450 580 650 663
COGS/Royalties (8) (19) (35) (55) (75) (95) (105)
SG&A (Commercial) (45) (60) (75) (90) (100) (110) (115)
R&D (Post-Mkt) (5) (5) (5) (5) (5) (5) (5)
EBIT (23) 11 70 160 270 370 425
Tax (21%) 0 (2) (14) (33) (56) (77) (89)
NOPAT (23) 9 56 127 214 293 336
FCF (25) 8 55 125 210 290 330
PV of FCF (20) 6 36 73 110 136 139 315

Using the above calculations, we calculated the risk-adjusted net present value (rNPV) for Anaphylm as $687.6 million, or $5.09/share.

Libervant:

Libervant is expected to be launched in the U.S. by 2027. We used a 95% PoS.

Libervant Free Cash Flow Forecast (Risk-Adjusted)

Year 2026E 2027E 2028E 2029E 2030E Terminal
Revenue ($M) 0 20 50 90 125
EBIT Margin N/A 15% 35% 45% 50%
FCF (5) 2 15 35 55
PV of FCF (4.5) 1.6 10.8 22.6 31.2 145

Using above calculations, we value Libervant as $196.4 million or $1.45/share.

Legacy Portfolio:

We value the legacy portfolio, like Suboxone, Sympazan, and Azstarys royalty streams, at $115 million or $0.85/share.

After adjusting for cash, debt, and corporate overhead (deduction of $150 million), we value Aquestive shares at $6.91/share.

Valuation Summary:

Asset Component Value ($M) Per Share Value ($) Contribution
Anaphylm (rNPV) $687.6 $5.09 63%
Libervant (rNPV) $196.4 $1.45 18%
Legacy Commercial $115.0 $0.85 11%
Net Cash & Other $(66.0)$ $(0.49)$ (6%)
Total Equity Value $933.0 $6.91 100%

Sensitivity Matrix:

We performed a sensitivity analysis on two key variables: WACC and Anaphylm peak sales.

WACC \ Peak Sales $450M $650M (Base) $850M
10.0% $5.85 $7.85 $9.95
11.5% $5.10 $6.91 $8.65
13.0% $4.45 $5.95 $7.45

  • Base Case: 11.5% WACC / $650M Peak Sales = $6.91.

  • Bear Case: 13.0% WACC / $450M Peak Sales = $4.45.

  • Bull Case: 10.0% WACC / $850M Peak Sales = $9.95.

Even in a bearish scenario, assuming a 13% discount rate and peak sales lower by 30% of our estimate, the shares are valued at $4.45, which is still higher than the current price.

Financial Strength:

Aquestive had $129 million in cash reserves at the end of Q3 2025. Long term debt was $29.8 million at Q3 205 end. The operating cash use was $50.5 million in the last 12 months, or ~$13 million. The company is expected to have $110 million at present considering above operating cash use. This provides a cash runway into 2027. The company also has a $75 million strategic funding agreement with RTW Investments on Anaphylm approval (non-dilutive financing). I don't expect a capital raise before Anaphylm approval.

4. Risks in This Investment

Despite our calculations, there is a risk of unexpected delay in Anaphylm approval if the FDA does not accept the NDA resubmission (though its probability is low). Anaphylm is also in a crowded market with several established competitors, though it has advantages mentioned above. Most of the valuation is assigned to Anaphylm, so any delay in its approval or failure to gain market share would compromise our thesis.

5. Final Recommendation

Investors have dumped Aquestive shares, and the shares are mispriced. We expect a successful NDA resubmission within 6 months and approval in H1 2027. We consider it one of the most compelling contrarian opportunities in the small cap pharma space with a 12-month upside of 92%. Rating AQST a 'Buy' with a 12-month price target of $7.

data.title: Data Update 6 For 2026: In Search Of Profitability
data.description:
Crass and mercantile though this may sound, the endgame for a business is to make money, and a business that fails this simple test cannot survive for long, no matter how noble its social mission, how great its products, and how much they are loved by its customers and employees. In this post, I start with a defense of this mercantile objective and argue that attempts to expand it to incorporate social good leave both businesses and societies worse off. I look at business profitability, first in absolute terms in 2025, and then relative to revenues, examining why profit margins vary across businesses and sectors. I then raise the ante and argue that making money is too low a standard to hold companies to, since the capital invested in these companies can generate returns elsewhere, opening the door to bringing in the opportunity costs (costs of equity and capital) that I introduced in my last post.

The Business Endgame

In 1970, Milton Friedman argued in a New York Times article that the social responsibility of a business is to deliver (and increase) profits. That view has come under attack in recent decades, but even in the immediate aftermath of the article’s appearance, there was some pushback. Some came from people who argued that Friedman was missing details, with a few noting that it is cash flows, not earnings, that businesses should focus on, and others arguing that it is profits over the long term, not just immediate profits, that should be the focus of a business. My guess is that Professor Friedman would have agreed on both fronts, arguing that he was talking about economic, not accounting, profits, and that there was nothing in his mission statement that foreclosed a focus on long-term profits.

In the decades since, there has been a more fundamental critique of the Friedman business endgame, coming from those who believe that his view is far too cramped and narrow a vision for a business and that businesses have obligations to society and the planet that need to be incorporated into decision-making. Initially, these critics argued for imposing social and environmental constraints on the profitability objective, and while Friedman may have taken issue with some of these constraints, arguing that that is what laws and regulations should be doing, he would (probably) have gone along with most of them, given real-world frictions. Later, though, these critics decided to go for the jugular, arguing that the business objective itself be reframed to include these broader responsibilities, with some arguing for stakeholder wealth maximization, where businesses seek to maximize value to their different stakeholders (employees, lenders, customers). That idea gained traction among some academics, many of whom never grappled with putting this objective into practice in real businesses, and among some CEOs, who realized that being accountable to everyone effectively meant being accountable to no one, but I am not a fan. About two decades ago, stakeholder wealth maximization was supplemented by ESG, an acronym that quickly got buy-in from the establishment. In 2020, when I first looked at ESG, it was at the height of its allure, with investment managers (led by Blackrock), consultants (with McKinsey up front), and academics, all pushing for its adoption. Given the broad buy-in, I expected to see clear and conclusive evidence that ESG was not just good for investors and businesses, but also for society, and I was disappointed on every front. The alpha that was attributed to ESG in investing was accidental, coming almost entirely from its overload on tech stocks in its early years, the evidence that ESG helped businesses deliver higher growth and profits was laughably weak, and on almost every societal dimension that ESG was supposed to make the world a better place, it had failed. Even on risk, the one dimension where a rational argument can be mounted for companies following the ESG rulebook, its impact was hazy, with no discernible effects on costs of capital and only anecdotal (and mostly ex post) evidence for protecting against reputational and catastrophic risks. In the last five years, ESG has fallen out of favor, largely undone by its own internal inconsistencies, but the gravy train that lived off its largesse has moved on and taken much of what filled the ESG space, repackaged it, and renamed it sustainability. While advocates for sustainability try to create distance between ESG and sustainability, in my (biased) view, much of that discussion is akin to painting lipstick on a pig and then debating what shade of lipstick suits the pig best, rather than attempting to create real change.

It is with intent, therefore, that I named these three forces - stakeholder wealth maximization, ESG and sustainability - the theocratic trifecta in a post that I wrote three years ago, and argued that they failed for the same reasons.

First, by rooting themselves in virtue rather than in business sense, they rendered a disservice to their own cause. After all, once you decide that you are on the side of goodness, any critics of what you do, no matter how well merited their criticism might be, are quickly consigned to the badness heap, and not just ignored, but also reviled for lacking moral fibre. The problem, of course, is that if an action makes business sense (increases profitability and value), you would not need a virtue brigade to push for that action in the first place. Second, by leaving the definitions of their central ideas (stakeholder wealth, ESG and sustainability) amorphous, they made it easier to sell to investors and companies, but at the expense of consistency and focus. In my 2022 post on ESG, where the Russian invasion of Ukraine had forced its defenders to morph in the face of evidence that world was more dependent on fossil fuels and defense companies than they had been willing to concede in earlier years, I noted the loss of credibility that comes from shifting definitions of goodness. Third, and most critically, in their zeal to push these concepts to a wider audience and get more people to buy in, they sold a lie, i.e., that you can be good (whatever that definition of good may be) without sacrifice. I have no idea whether ESG and sustainability salespeople meant what they said when they argued that investors could earn higher returns, by adding ESG constraints to their portfolios, and that companies could become more profitable, if they incorporated environmental and social considerations into decision-making, but my categorization of people in these spaces as either useful idiots or feckless knaves stems from a refusal to face up to the inherent trade-offs.

After decades of pushback from critics of the Friedman business endgame, I, for one, believe that Milton Friedman was right, and that we would all be better off to follow up and ask the question of what can be done, given that businesses are profit-seekers, to advance social good and curb externalities. I don't believe that the disclosure route, which seems to have become the fallback for some seeking better business behavior, will accomplish much, and it may do more harm than good. While laws and regulations can provide a partial fix, they are blunt instruments, and in a setting where businesses can move easily across borders, they may not be effective. Ultimately, we (as consumers and voters) get the businesses we deserve, and if after paying lip service to social causes, we buy products and vote for governments that undercut those causes, no acronym or word salad will repair the breach.

Profitability in Businesses

I meant to have a short lead-in on why profitability matters at businesses, but as you can see from the previous section, I did get sidetracked, but the underlying message is that making money is central to business success and survival, and that measuring profitability is therefore a necessary part of assessing business success and value.

Economic versus Accounting Profits

The Friedman view on the business endgame may have been driven by a vision of economic profits, but in the real world, we are dependent on accounting measures of profits, which are, at best, imperfect substitutes for economic profits. The table below looks at an accounting income statement, highlighting the many measures of profits - gross, operating and net - that you will find in it:

Each profit measure has utility, with gross profits reflecting unit economics, the difference between gross and operating profits capturing economies of scale, and the difference between operating and net profits being driven by taxes and choices that businesses make on debt and non-operating assets. In 2025, looking at the aggregate values (in millions of US $) for these line items across sectors, here are the numbers, for both global firms and just the US subset:

In the aggregate, global firms generated $6.2 trillion in net income and $7.7 trillion in operating income on revenues of $72.4 trillion, in 2025; during the same year, US firms generated $2.2 trillion in net income and $2.9 trillion in operating income on revenues of $22.7 trillion. Across sectors, and looking at revenues, industrials carried the most weight for the global sample, but health care generated the most revenues across the US sub-sample.

Profits scaled to Revenues - Profit Margins

The problem with dollar profits is that comparisons across companies, industries, or sectors are skewed by scale differences, and one simple scalar for earnings is revenues, yielding variants of profit margins. While you are undoubtedly familiar with these margin variants, their real use in analysis is in providing insight into business models.

I am not a believer in financial ratio analysis, but I do believe that the income statements for companies, especially examined over time, give us insight into their business models and can help frame valuation narratives. In the table below, I look at differences in margins across sectors in 2025, again looking across global firms, and just US firms:

I have estimated margins, by sector, using the aggregated dollar values for profits and revenues from the previous table, and also reported the cross-sectional distribution of company-level margins. Comparing the aggregated margin with the median margin across the sector should give you a sense of how top-heavy the sector is in terms of profitability. In technology, which has the highest weighted operating margin (24.7%) across sectors, the median operating margin is only 3.41% (-0.30%) across global (US) technology firms; the bigger tech companies are money machines in a sector that still contains a lot of younger and smaller money-losing firms. Note that the margins are not computed for financial services firms, since revenues are often unreported (and mostly meaningless) and gross and operating profits don't have the same measurement value as they do for non-financial services firms.

Industry Margins and the AI Threat

Breaking down sectors into industries provides more granular detail, and there is a link at the bottom of this post that reports the margin statistics, by industry group. At the risk of stating the obvious, there are large disparities in margins across industries, reflecting differences in unit economics, economies of scale and leverage, as can be seen in this table that lists the industry groupings with the highest and lowest aggregated operating margins among US firms:

At one end of the spectrum, you have industry groups like basic chemicals, which have an aggregated (median) gross margin of 9.31%, making the margin hill much steeper to climb since operating margins and net margins will be lower. At the other end of the spectrum, in addition to tobacco and railroads (surprised, right?), you have system and application software, delivering an aggregated gross margin of 71.72%, operating margin of 33.21% and net margins of 25.49%, capturing the strong unit economics that characterize the business.

While high margins are a desirable feature for a business, these same high margins can make a business vulnerable to disruption, and the AI sell-off that we have seen play out in the last few months in software reflects the concerns that investors have of AI putting significant downward pressure on software margins. If your pushback is that the drop-off in revenues and margins has not happened yet, and that it is unfair to software firms to mark their market pricing down preemptively, this is exactly what markets are supposed to do, and these software companies benefited earlier in their lives, when market prices were marked up well ahead of the run-up in margins. You live by the sword (expectations of growth and high margins), you die by it (expectations that growth rates will hit a cliff and margins will decline)!

Time Trends in Profits

I have tracked profit margins for companies for a long time (about three decades) in my datasets, and there is clear evidence that they have trended upwards during the period. In the graph below, I look at the net profit margins for the S&P 500 in the aggregate in this century (from 2000-2025):

As you can see, net profit margins have climbed over the last two decades for US companies, with a number of stories competing for why.

  • The most cynical explanation is that this increase in margins is all sleight-of-hand, where accountants are pushing through changes, aided and abetted by accounting rule-writers, to make companies look more profitable. As someone who has taken issue with the gaming of earnings that you often see at companies, I am disinclined to take this criticism seriously, since many of the changes in accounting rules (such as the expensing of stock-based compensation and R&D) should push earnings down, and accountants have more power to move income across periods than they do to increase the level of income.
  • A second explanation is that the macroeconomic environment makes it easier for companies to deliver profits, and this explanation had resonance when interest rates were at historic lows in the last decade. As rates have risen back to more normal levels and the economy limps along, I am skeptical of the reasoning in this explanation.
  • A third explanation, and this one has been eagerly adopted by many on the political left, is that this reflects the increase in bargaining power for capital, relative especially to labor, implying that the increase in profits is coming primarily at the expense of worker wages. While there are certainly pockets of the economy where this is true, the margins for most manufacturing and service businesses, which have the highest employee count and wage costs, have stagnated or decreased over the last 20 years, indicating that neither capital nor labor has benefited at least in these sectors.
  • The fourth, and in my view the most salient rationale for margin increases, is that the composition of the market has changed, as technology companies supplant old-economy companies, bringing superior unit economics and economies of scale to play. Put simply, a market that gets the largest portion of its value from tech companies will deliver much higher margins that one that gets much of its value from manufacturing and service businesses.

Should we be concerned that margins may compress in the future? Of course, and we always should, but that compression, if it happens, will depend almost entirely on how the economy performs and the effects of disruption, if it is coming, for tech companies.

Value Creation in Business

If we define the threshold for business success as generating profits, we are setting the bar too low for a simple reason. Starting a business requires capital, and that capital can earn a return elsewhere on investment of equivalent risk. If those words sound familiar, it is because I used them in my last post on hurdle rates to describe the costs of equity and capital. Thus, value creation requires a business to generate a return on its equity (capital) that exceeds its cost of equity (capital). That is a simple proposition, and a powerful one, but the measurement challenge we face is in determining the returns that companies generate, and for better or worse, we are dependent on accounting measures of these returns. A good way to see what an accounting return is measuring or at least trying to measure is to look at returns on equity and invested capital in a financial balance sheet:

While accounting returns are widely used in practice as a gauge of investment quality, they can be skewed not just by accounting inconsistencies but efforts by accountants to do the "right thing" (like writing off bad investments) I have laid out my concerns in exhaustive and incredibly boring detail in this paper on accounting returns, which is dated, but still relevant. I summarize the factors that can cause accounting returns on equity and capital to deviate from reality in the picture below:

With those concerns about accounting returns in place, I computed the accounting returns on equity and invested capital for all of the companies in my global sample (48,156 firms) and my US sample (5,994 firms), and the following table reports the statistics for both groups, by sector:

Again, I report the accounting returns computed based on aggregated values first, and then the distributional statistics (first quartile, median, third quartile) for the company-level accounting returns. As with profit margins, you can see that even in sectors where the aggregated accounting returns are high (such as technology and communication services), the median value reflects the reality that most companies in these sectors struggle to deliver double-digit returns.

Turning back to our value creation metric, where we compare accounting returns to costs of equity and capital, you have to be consistent, comparing equity returns to equity costs and capital returns to capital costs:

The excess return is a number, but as with all numbers in business, it is worth looking behind the number at its drivers, i.e., why do some businesses deliver returns that consistently outstrip their costs of equity and capital, whereas others struggle? The most powerful explainer of excess returns is not qualitative, since the capacity to generate excess returns comes from barriers to entry and competitive advantages. In the language of value investing, it is the width (strength of competitive advantages) and depth (sustainability of competitive advantage) of moats that determine whether a company can earn more than its cost of equity or capital:

If you are interested in this topic, and it is a fascinating one, Michael Mauboussin brings his erudition and knowledge into play in this Morgan Stanley thought piece from October 2024.

Since I have estimates of costs of equity and capital for each of my firms (see my last data update for details), I compute excess returns, by sector, for my global and US samples:

Given what you saw in the last table, with accounting returns, you should not be surprised to learn that only 29% (28%) of global firms earn returns on equity (capital) that exceed their costs of equity (capital). In fact, if you raise the threshold and look at companies that generate 5% or more as excess returns, the numbers drop off to 19% (17%) for equity (capital) excess returns. Most companies have trouble earning their costs of equity and capital, but if you look at the aggregated values, there are multiple sectors in the US (technology, consumer goods and communication services) that earn double-digit excess returns, pointing again to larger companies within these sectors being able to set themselves apart from the rest.

If your concern is that the global statistics are being skewed by regional differences, I compute the excess return statistics broken down by region:

As you can see, there is not a single geography where more than 50% of firms earn more than their required returns, with Japan ranking highest in percentages and Canada and Australia the lowest. Here again, the aggregated values tell a different story, with US companies collectively delivering excess returns of 8.44% on equity and 1.81% on capital, suggesting again that large US companies carry the weight of value creation in the market.

Given how much time we spend in finance examining investments and developing decision rules (NPV>0, IRR>Hurdle rate) that are supposed to protect businesses from taking "bad" investments, you may be surprised at the prevalence of value-destroying investments. Some of the failure at businesses to deliver returns on capital that exceed the cost of capital may reflect imperfections in our accounting return measures, since it is based upon earnings in the most recent year, and that may bias us against young and growing companies building up to scale. In my book on the corporate life cycle, I highlight how accounting returns shift as companies go from youth to decline:

To see if this is a factor in our global findings on excess returns, I break companies down by age into deciles and compute excess returns across these groupings:

The table broadly reflects what you should expect to see, with a corporate life cycle, as the percent of companies that beat their cost of capital increases as companies age, but the aggregated excess returns peak in middle age (the middle of the life cycle), more pronounced with US than global firms.

A Profitability Wrap-Up

Looking at the data, and there is a danger here that I am overreaching, it seems to me that over the last four decades, moats have crumbled, partly as a result of global competition and partly because of disruption (which upends businesses, turning good businesses into bad ones), and the business landscape has tilted more decisively to larger firms, as more and more businesses become winner(s)-take-all. It is in this context that I take a more jaundiced view of what AI will do for company profitability and value. I believe that, as a disruptor, it will cause downward pressure on margins at most firms, and increase the advantages that larger firms have in each business. How do I reconcile this view with the happy talk of AI as a tool that will make companies more productive, and that the resulting lower costs will make them more profitable? Unless the AI tools that you are talking about are exclusive to these companies, in the sense that competitors cannot buy the same or equivalent tools, these AI tools will lower costs across the board, and competition will then kick in on the pricing front, lowering profitability. If that sounds like a reach, I would recommend a revisit of the US retail sector over the last three decades, as online retail, initially viewed as a boon by brick-and-mortar retail firms, ended up destroying most of them and reducing the margins for retail collectively. As consumers, we will benefit, but as investors or employees in the disrupted companies, we will pay a price that outweighs the benefits for a sizable number of us. I do think that the AI disruption will be more akin to a slow-motion car wreck, in terms of its effect on overall profitability, and that the margin slippage will occur over time, but it will be damaging. Time will tell!

Datasets

  1. Profit margins, by industry (US and Global)
  2. Accounting returns and excess returns, by industry (US and Global)

Paper on Accounting Returns (Long and Boring)

  1. Return on Capital, Return on Invested Capital and Return on Equity: Measurement and Implications

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ECC And OXLC: Highway To The Danger Zone
data.description:
If our brains were simple enough for us to understand them, we'd be so simple that we couldn't.”

Source: Ian Stewart, The Collapse of Chaos: Discovering Simplicity in a Complex World

If making 10% on income investing was actually easy, income investors would still blow up capital chasing 20% returns.

Source: Trapping Value

On our last coverage on Eagle Point Credit Company Inc. (ECC), we went over the total return profile and why the net investment income (NII) told you absolutely nothing of value. We concluded our piece with the following:

They also have outperformed the common shares by a mile and we think that outperformance continues as ECC likely goes below $5.25 in the coming weeks. All that said, we are not interested in either at present as the NAV deterioration is just too steep for us to sleep well at night.

Source: Yield Of Dreams, NAV Drops 11% In A Month

Well, this morning, we were here. $4.00.

Previous to that piece, we had said this about the ECC distribution:

Nonetheless, a cut is highly probable soon, and ECC gets the maximum risk of a cut fairly soon on our proprietary Kenny Loggins scale.

The big risk is that this stays at a permanent discount to NAV and takes away a good exit point for the yield chasers.

Source: CLO Myth Comes To Haunt The Income Chasers

The distribution was cut this morning by 57% (6 cents a month from 14 cents a month). We go over some thoughts on the leverage profile here and where the trade is now set up.

Results

ECC started off by mentioning the NII once more.

Net investment income (“NII”) of $0.23 per weighted average common share.

NII less realized losses from investments of negative $0.26 per weighted average common share

Source: ECC Press Release

Funnily enough, we did see what NII less realized losses was, and this number probably shocked the "just temporary market conditions" crowd. Management comments reflected confidence and a newfound aspiration of maximizing total returns.

“During the fourth quarter, we continued our disciplined focus on portfolio management and long-term value creation through CLO resets and refinancings. These actions helped mitigate some of the headwinds faced by CLO equity this past year,” said Thomas P. Majewski, Chief Executive Officer. “We also leveraged our Adviser’s broader investment origination capabilities, opportunistically increasing the Company’s exposure to credit asset classes beyond CLO equity. During the fourth quarter, we deployed $184 million of capital, of which approximately $147 million was in such investments. This strategy complements our core CLO equity portfolio and reflects an intentional decision to focus on maximizing total return for our shareholders.

Source: ECC Press Release (emphasis ours)

We did not find any reference to "gobs of cash" this time around from management. After years of 20% yields and sub-5% total returns, "gobs of cash," is likely being retired from the lexicon. The company estimated that its NAV dropped from $7.00 at the end of September 2025 to $5.70 at the end of December 2025. It had a further decline at the end of January to around $5.44 to $5.54 (range provided by the company). It also launched a joint venture with a strategic investment partner who will focus on acquiring regulatory capital relief investments.

Outlook

ECC's comment about total returns is fairly interesting, as the fund has delivered less than 3.5% annualized since inception.
