There is a fairly large spread to NAV today, but even assuming that NAV is hit (before it declines further), total annualized returns barely improve to 5%. Those are awful "risk-adjusted" numbers. But aren't CLO equity investments supposed to do better? Well, kind of. There is a lot of misinformation on the sector, and the main issue is the lumping of secured loans, CLO debt, and CLO equity, all into one common asset class. ECC itself compares the fund to the Leveraged Loan Index and S&P BDC Index when it is a fairly different beast than either of these two.

CLO equity management is far more complicated, and the picture below shows the big difference in CLO equity returns across their Universe. The 25th and 75th percentile performers differ sometimes by as much as 15% of IRR.

So manager skill is fairly important, and we are seeing that just buying for yield does not work. We are seeing this in most (if not all) public CLO equity CEFs. Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC) announced their own NAV this morning, which fell over 11% from the previous month. Last time we wrote about that, we expressed concern that the NAV had fallen 19% in one quarter. So these drops are fairly steep, and anyone chasing buy after buy is losing the plot (and their shirt).

Getting back to our protagonist, ECC also has to deal with the fact that its secondary instruments now are starving for required capital coverage. We are referring to these.

In there are the baby bonds like Eagle Point Credit Company Inc. 6.6875% NT 28 (ECCX) and the preferred shares like Eagle Point Credit Company Inc. 6.75% PFD SR D (ECC.PR.D). According to ECC, both are secured by a 200% asset coverage requirement (some funds use a higher, self-imposed, requirement for baby bonds versus preferred shares). But for ECC, the overall 200% matters for all senior securities. On the last check, the coverage was at 2.31X based on January 31, 2026, numbers.

While that may sound comforting, ECC is 14% away from there being a test to this coverage requirement. That is, if the NAV drops 14% (which looks like it could happen in a 30-day period), ECC will be forced to redeem some senior securities and likely liquidate assets at really bad prices.

Verdict

Before you forget, ECC is not exactly buying triple A securities. In fact, more than 60% of the total invested capital is in B or lower securities.

Despite the much-touted resilience of CLOs, we know that these can go to hell in a handbasket in a recession. The required capital buffer used to be far higher in 2024 (about 2.67X as shown below).

So ECC needs to change something drastically, including perhaps hiring new managers. Whether that happens or not remains to be seen. In the interim, investors should take the hard lessons from this drop. The most important in our view is to stop throwing capital on a bad investment. Yes, we all make mistakes, but there is no reason to keep buying at every step as the NAV evaporates. Here, a mea culpa is also in order. While we were "less wrong" than the majority, we did not foresee such a big drop in NAVs for OXLC and ECC. So we missed the multiple opportunities to warn investors even more strongly than we actually did. We will try and do better next time.

While we have been interested in the baby bonds and preferreds in the past, the current NAV declines mean that the yields offered on these securities still appear low to us. We are staying out of those as well.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it or sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: Ethereum At Risk Of Falling Under $996
data.description:
My forecast that Bitcoin (BTC-USD) would plunge to $60,000 after the January FOMC meeting played out as expected. That is, BTC gained significant downward momentum on January 30, 2026, two days after the meeting, and reached a low of $59,930 on February 6, 2026.

With that move and my first target of $60,000 now realized, my broader bearish thesis remains intact, and I continue to target BTC at $30,000 by the end of this crypto winter. For Ethereum (ETH-USD), the corresponding bottom should be below $996, as I’ll demonstrate in this article.

Core Macro Trends Still Firm

I remain bearish because the sector rotation out of crypto (risk-off sentiment), resulting from the normalization of both real yields and liquidity (discussed in my articles here and here), is still in play and will remain so unless there are major macroeconomic changes – a highly unlikely scenario for the foreseeable future given the Fed’s latest economic projections that indicate robust growth, benign inflation, and normal unemployment.

Below are two charts; one compares ETH prices against real yield changes (referred to as RYC going forward) and money supply (M2) changes (referred to as M2C).

These two charts clearly depict ETH’s inverse relation to RYC and direct relationship to M2C, albeit with a lag, supporting my view that normalization of these two broader macroeconomic metrics is the primary cause of the crypto winter that we are in at present.

I won’t, however, go into the details of BTC’s and, by extension, ETH’s relation to RYC and M2C because I’ve already done so in my articles linked above. If you haven’t read those, I recommend that you do so before continuing.

A Discrepancy

Next, consider the chart below, which compares ETH’s prices against BTC’s since November 2017.

Although BTC and ETH demonstrate a strong statistical correlation, a major discrepancy is observable. Bitcoin’s second all-time high of $124,752 was nearly twice its previous peak of $67,566, whereas Ethereum’s second high at $4,831 exceeded its earlier top of $4,812 by only ~0.39%.

Uncovering the reasons for this discrepancy is the crux of my analysis and will eventually allow me to set a price of price target of less than $996.

Peaks and Troughs

Before the two crypto bull runs since 2019, ETH and BTC had bottomed out on January 29, 2019, at $105 and $3,448, respectively.

Following that, on November 8, 2021, BTC attained its then-all-time high of $67,566, while ETH reached its peak at $4,812. The fact that ETH appreciated forty-five times from its bottom, whereas BTC appreciated “only” nineteen times, indicates that forces other than a risk-on sentiment were in play for ETH.

To be specific, ETH’s peak in 2021 was in large part due to the DeFi summer, characterized by an explosion of decentralized finance apps and, consequently, a surge in on-chain transaction volume that caused not only gas prices to explode (chart below) but also an indirect demand for ETH for its use as collateral in lending and liquidity pools.

Furthermore, testing for the Beacon chain commenced on December 1, 2020, which was a strong indicator that moving to Proof of Stake was no longer just talk but a reality.

You don’t need to understand the technicalities behind that change, only that the Beacon test decisively signaled that a major reduction in the ETH supply is coming. And as with most things in life, when supply decreases and demand remains elevated, as it did during the DeFi summer, prices rise.

Then came the crypto crash, led by changes in RYC and M2C expectations, causing ETH to fall to $993 on June 18, 2022, and BTC’s drop to $16,439 on December 19, 2022. ETH crashed earlier than BTC due to the collapse of Celsius Network and Three Arrows Capital. I won’t go into the details of those failures because they have no bearing on my thesis.

As for tackling the congestion crisis caused by DeFi summer, work on it began with the introduction of fee burning through the EIP-1559 Implementation on August 5, 2021, but the problem was not completely resolved until the Dencun upgrade in March 2024. That is, with the Dencun upgrade, transaction fees on Layer 2 dropped by 90-95%, eventually causing ETH prices to crash.

The primary takeaway here is that it was not until March 2024 that congestion and fee effects were priced out, meaning, on a fundamental level, any high or low before that was larger than what it should have been if ETH were to exist in the state that it does now. That explains why, during the second crypto rally, ETH’s prices were only marginally higher than its previous high.

With that occurrence and the logic behind it taken into consideration, it’s also reasonable to assume that ETH's trough will also only be marginally higher than at the end of that first crypto rally. As mentioned before, the trough was at $993, which means that ETH's new fundamental floor is ~$996.92 (1.0039 multiplied by $993).

Why not $1,500 witnessed in March 2024 as the base? Because at that time, risk-on sentiment due to elevated RYC and MC2 was prevalent – not the case at present or at the time of the $993 trough, making the latter a better benchmark.

Trending Towards Net Inflation

Note that in the previous section I did not claim that price magnification due to the Beacon upgrade, and thus a net deflationary supply, has also been priced out. That's because ETH until now has remained net deflationary, but I suspect that to change in the coming months.

I claim so based on ETH’s supply trend for the past few years (chart below) and the early signal it's providing, i.e., a move from a state of deflation (issuance less than burn – supply contraction) to inflation (issuance greater than burn – excess supply).

That said, average and total ETH burned per year have also demonstrated a downtrend (charts below). Thus, it remains to be seen whether ETH will reach a state of net inflation, and more importantly, whether that will lead to meaningful changes in prices.

Also, if you believe that ETH cannot enter a net inflation stage, you would be wrong in your assumption, because the event has already happened once, in April 2025; see the chart below.

Also note that the average burn rate in that week, at 91 per day, was much higher than the average burn rate of 56 per day that has been observed YTD 2026.

An argument against all that has been presented in this section could be that ETH prices rallied even when it was inflationary, i.e., before the introduction of fee burn and Proof of Stake.

That’s a valid argument, but I’m not saying that ETH cannot rise in an inflationary situation, only that its price will and should adjust if it turns net inflationary as opposed to net deflationary.

Stated differently, since September 16, 2022, when ETH became net deflationary for the first time, it has been treated as a scarce asset, arguably more so than BTC because the latter’s supply is fixed, not decreasing. But if and when there are indications that ETH’s supply may increase for the foreseeable future, its price should be altered to incorporate that fact.

Thesis Risk: New Projects

An argument against, and the primary risk to my thesis, is that the burn rate may rise due to new and promising ETH projects in 2026 and beyond. While that would be a fair argument, the likelihood of such an event remains low because KPMG’s Venture Pulse Report for Q4 2025 states that most venture capital money is flowing into AI startups.

Nonetheless, total money invested in pre-seed startups, a category that all new blockchain startups will fall under, is larger than it was in 2020, at the height of the DeFi summer. However, a reasonable assumption is that AI startups are the primary beneficiaries of all those early-stage funds.

Also, a risk-off sentiment, which in turn is dictated by RYC and M2C, also affects VC funding activities because it is among the riskiest of all investments. This is to say that numbers presented in that report should not be taken at face value because 2026 brings a new liquidity regime where VC funding may move towards late-stage startups that are deemed less risky.

Last but not least, L2 (Layer-2) of ETH was implemented such that another DeFi summer induced congestion event does not ever occur, indicating that it would take a revolutionary ETH-based project to increase burn rate.

Closing Thoughts

The big takeaway of this entire thesis is that investors should not jump on the ETH bandwagon when this crypto season is over, because its lowest value, while most likely to be less than $996, is hard to determine with all the unknown variables at present.

I also want to state that the next crypto crash is unlikely to occur following the next FOMC meeting, as markets have already priced in the expectation that there will be few, if any, rate cuts in 2026 – particularly after a 130K increase in January 2026 payrolls and a January CPI print of 2.4%, suggesting that downside risks to both sides of the Fed’s dual mandate are diminishing at the current Fed Funds rate, eliminating the need to tweak it.

That said, I expect both BTC and ETH to drop to new lows of ~$55,000 and ~$1,500, before bouncing back to their respective floors of $60,000 and $1,500, after the next Fed meeting on March 18, 2026.

Mainly because Chairman Powell, in the last FOMC meeting, clearly stated that the Fed will assess incoming data to determine whether another rate cut is required. Colloquially speaking, another decision to hold rates steady will be the final indicator for a rate cut pause, but as of now the market cannot know for sure and will assume otherwise until it's official.

When that temporary collapse and bounce back does happen, most media outlets will term it as the end of the crypto winter, and the narrative will most likely move from the sentiment at present that “crypto is in freefall, don’t buy” to “markets have bottomed, time to buy again.” But that would be a false claim, and I advise readers not to enter a position at those prices.

data.title: Danaos: Alaska LNG Investment - Smart Diversification Or Opportunity Cost?
data.description:
Danaos Corporation (DAC) is now trading over the $100 share price ceiling. If you’ve followed me covering the stock in recent years, you know this moment was long overdue. I’ve covered the company 13 times since my first piece on it on Seeking Alpha in Sep 2021, which was titled "Danaos: About To Sail To $100 And Beyond". It did in 2022, but then spent years trying to get back up.

Of course, there were times I’ve been frustrated with DAC's performance, like in this April 2024 article, when I made the case for finding it appropriate to take the exit. However, I’ve generally remained bullish and consistently supported that DAC is deeply undervalued. So finally seeing it move back above $100 again makes me feel like the world is in the right place.

What's making the update I'm writing now quite special is that Danaos has decided to sail far from its home waters (containership and drybulks) to the LNG space. That's quite a major change for a company I have always thought of as a pure containership play (with a bit of dry bulk exposure after it entered the space a few years ago).

We already know the containership fleet is set to keep generating predictable revenue for years to come. Apart from CapEx to recycle the fleet over time, I would have expected the company to accelerate capital returns. Hopefully, that would also re-rate the stock higher. Now this investment changes the whole conversation.

Core Operations

Before I discuss the LNG investment, let me briefly touch on the core business. There is nothing surprising here. Quarterly revenues reached a new record of around $266 million, driven by the containership fleet being effectively fully booked at excellent rates. You can easily expect more of the same here over the medium term, as 100% of 2026 operating days and 87% of 2027 days are already locked in at highly profitable rates. The contracted revenue backlog has now reached a huge $4.3 billion, providing envy-worthy earnings visibility.

I also owe some credit to the dry bulk segment, which I had previously viewed as a distraction. I would still prefer if Danaos had stuck with containership, but at least we know the dry bulk vessels are contributing to profits. The division posted adjusted EBITDA of $12.9 million, nearly doubling YoY following near-perfect 99.8% utilization.

The balance remained pristine in the meantime, with Danaos' cash position surpassing $1 billion and net leverage at a tiny 0.20x Net Debt/EBITDA. The key question, therefore, continues to revolve around where all this cash will go.

Danaos can continue to expand/renew its fleet using cash from operations, and, as many other analysts and I have argued here on Seeking Alpha for years, the stock remains deeply undervalued. Will we finally see accelerated buybacks or a tender offer at these levels? Well, management had a different idea. Let's examine it.

The Alaska LNG Move

The answer to where Danaos can best deploy its cash pile arrived a few days before its Q4 results. In late January, the company announced a $50 million strategic investment in the Alaska LNG project through a partnership with Glenfarne Group, effectively marking its entry into the LNG space. Under the deal, Danaos becomes the "preferred tonnage provider" for at least six LNG carriers required for the project.

What is the rationale for this move? I would assume that management feels that investing further in the containership sector, which will face a massive wave of newbuild deliveries in 2027, might lead to over-concentration. After all, Danaos already has many more newbuilds hitting the water through 2026-2028, and all of them have been locked in at exceptional rates, some running as far away as 2038.

The LNG investment will allow Danaos to diversify into another area, where charters typically feature even longer multi-decade terms that resemble long-term infrastructure bonds. You can take a look at Capital Clean Energy Carriers (CCEC) as an example. When I covered it back in October, I mentioned how its time-charter book extended to 118 years (not a typo) if all options were to be exercised, precisely because of how long these contracts are.

But does this move add risk to the business? I think so. In the news release, Danaos said it will "operate" these vessels. In shipping, "operate" comes with assuming technical management of the vessel. Danaos will thus be responsible for the specialized crews and complex maintenance required to transport LNG. Danaos knows what it’s doing when it comes to managing container ships; I don't question that. But LNG might prove to be different. I don't really know too much about these procedures, but I can imagine that dealing with crews, maintenance, insurance, and the safety standards that come with handling gas at -162°C sounds like a different game that requires different expertise.

At the very least, there is a point to make that the move into LNG can once again be a distraction from the core business. We know the management is top-tier in the container shipping space, so it's easy to argue that staying in their lane would be the better approach. But Danaos' management is likely looking for longevity here and ensuring they are building a business for the next few decades. After all, the bond-like multi-year cash flow visibility that can come from these LNG deals does follow the same logic they've been following with the containerships, particularly the newbuilds, which, as I mentioned earlier, have already been attached to decade-long contracts.

The Capital Allocation Debate

The main criticism I have about the LNG move has to do with the more general capital allocation rationale, particularly as Danaos trades at a still-depressed, in my opinion, valuation. I get that reasons might include diversifying away from an already very heavy exposure to containerships and/or focusing on building a company with a multi-decade operating horizon ahead, but when the opportunity cost of buying back your own stock is so wide, it warrants scrutiny.

Even if Danaos can achieve an optimistic low double-digit return on the LNG investment (which carries operating risks), it might pale in comparison to the return from buying back DAC stock. At a 0.5x P/B ratio, every dollar spent on a buyback yields an immediate, nearly risk-free return that is already much higher.

To be fair, management did repurchase $75.7 million worth of stock during the full year, up from $53.3 million in FY2024. And that is a decent amount for a $2 billion company. But why not more? They clearly don't want to "shrink" the company by contracting the balance sheet. Still, capital allocation remains a forever debate when it comes to Danaos.

What I Am Doing

I continue to like Danaos a lot, even though I am not currently holding the stock. I sold a put a few months ago at $85 in hopes of getting in at a superb price (yes, I am this greedy). The premium was around $5.50, so it was a great deal. Following the recent rally, I bought the put back at about 10% of its original value. However, I still want some exposure in the company, given that the price remains disconnected from book value. If there is a pullback in the near term, especially below $100/share, I will likely re-enter.

The LNG move, regardless of how it plays out, should remain a small part of the core business. Whether Danaos could have realized higher returns for shareholders by buying back stock more aggressively remains to be seen (and is likely), but at the very worst, this should be a boring, bond-like stake to go along with the massive container-ship charter backlog that has been fueling the bullish case for years and is set to do so for many years ahead.
